questions.prompt: A text says a package could not be delivered — pay a small customs fee through the link within 24 hours. What gives it away?
choices: The deadline and the tiny fee — urgency plus a small ask is the classic hook
Delivery companies never send texts
Real customs fees are always larger
Nothing — it is safest to pay small fees quickly
questions.explanation: Scams pair a countdown with an amount too small to think twice about. If you are expecting a package, check it in the courier's own app or site — never through a link in a message.

questions.prompt: An email from "tech support" says your mailbox is full and asks you to reply with your password to expand it. What should you do?
choices: Reply — tech support already manages your account anyway
Send an older password you no longer use much
Forward the email to colleagues to see if they got it too
Never send it — no real support team or company asks for your password
questions.explanation: Anyone who genuinely runs the system can manage it without your password. A request for a password or a sign-in code is itself the proof of a scam.

questions.prompt: A marketplace seller offers a phone at half price, but only if you skip the site and pay by bank transfer or gift cards today. What is the tell?
choices: Half price alone — good deals are always fake
The seller has good reviews, so the payment method does not matter
Leaving the platform and paying by untraceable means
A big discount is normal for a quick private sale — pay fast before someone else does
questions.explanation: The platform's payment system is what protects you. Scammers pull you off it because transfers and gift cards cannot be reversed. The discount is the bait; the payment method is the trap.

questions.prompt: A pop-up flashes "VIRUS DETECTED — call this number now." What is really happening?
choices: Your device found a virus and opened a support line
It is an ad-style scare page — real security warnings do not ask you to call anyone
The number is your device maker's official helpline
Ignoring it will let the virus spread
questions.explanation: Operating systems and browsers never show a phone number to call. Close the tab or restart the browser; nothing was infected by the pop-up itself. The scam happens on the phone call, where a stranger asks for remote access or payment.

questions.prompt: A voice message that sounds exactly like a relative asks for money urgently and begs you not to tell anyone. What is the safest response?
choices: Contact your relative yourself on the number you already have
Send the money — the voice is unmistakable
Reply in the same chat and ask the voice for details
Post about it publicly to warn others first
questions.explanation: Voices can now be convincingly imitated. Secrecy plus urgency is the oldest pressure trick there is. Verify through a channel you already trust — call the person back on their known number.
